summary: The anterior compartment of the leg is supplied by the deep peroneal nerve, and its injury causes foot drop.
The anterior compartment is supplied by the superficial peroneal nerve, and its injury causes claw toes.
The anterior compartment is supplied by the tibial nerve, and its injury causes pes cavus.
The dorsalis pedis artery is the continuation of the anterior tibial artery, palpated between extensor hallucis longus and extensor digitorum longus tendons.
The dorsalis pedis artery is the continuation of the posterior tibial artery, palpated behind the medial malleolus.
The dorsalis pedis artery is the continuation of the peroneal artery, palpated on the lateral side of the ankle.
The unhappy triad involves the anterior cruciate ligament, medial collateral ligament, and medial meniscus.
The unhappy triad involves the posterior cruciate ligament, lateral collateral ligament, and lateral meniscus.
The unhappy triad involves both cruciate ligaments and the patellar tendon.
The popliteus muscle unlocks the knee from full extension by internally rotating the femur on the fixed tibia.
The gastrocnemius muscle unlocks the knee by externally rotating the tibia.
The plantaris muscle unlocks the knee by internally rotating the tibia.
The cruciate ligaments are intracapsular but extrasynovial.
The cruciate ligaments are both intracapsular and intrasynovial.
The cruciate ligaments are extracapsular and extrasynovial.
The anterior cruciate ligament prevents anterior displacement of the tibia on the femur (positive anterior drawer test).
The anterior cruciate ligament prevents posterior displacement of the tibia on the femur.
The anterior cruciate ligament prevents lateral displacement of the tibia.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review knee joint and anterior leg compartment anatomy.
Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
